summary: Patrick McKenzie reviews his 2013 business year across four lines of business: Bingo Card Creator (maintenance mode, sales down 23%), Appointment Reminder (revenue doubled), consulting (quit mid-year), and productized consulting (~$62k revenue). Total disclosed Kalzumeus Software revenue was ~$112k with ~$60k profit, though Appointment Reminder numbers are kept private and now dominate his income. The year was marked by a severe cashflow crunch caused by poorly timed consulting exit coinciding with tax obligations, capital investments, and seasonal lows, which contributed to serious health problems. Despite the difficulties, he successfully transitioned from consulting to productized consulting, outsourced BCC operations, and set ambitious goals for 2014.
key_points: Quitting consulting was the right strategic decision but catastrophically poor in execution timing, creating a perfect storm cashflow crunch when tax liabilities, slow season, and capital investments all collided with zero consulting revenue

Appointment Reminder doubled revenue year-over-year despite receiving only ~4 weeks of full-time development effort, demonstrating product-market fit but also massive unrealized potential from lack of focus
Successfully outsourced BCC customer support and A/B testing, achieving ~$1,000/hour effective wage on the ~20 hours spent, proving that systematizing operations enables profitable neglect

Productized consulting generated ~$62k across books, courses, and workshops, validating the model of converting consulting expertise into scalable products that free you from the billable-hours treadmill
Fear of the unknown prevented hiring for Appointment Reminder, revealing that the bottleneck in growing a solo business is often psychological rather than financial or operational
money_quotes: I anticipate that phone call to take 5 minutes or less. $2,400 is approximately equal to an entire month's profits of BCC. Any questions?
It turns out that my Nagging Doubt Monster is an idiot and that my skills generalize impressively well up the sophistication chain.

Quitting a consultancy with no active engagements turns out to be really easy: all you have to do is stop saying Yes. Conversely, starting a consultancy is fairly easy if you're known to be good at something with high utility to potential clients, since all you have to do is stop saying No.
I was routinely selling new engagements at e.g. $30,000 per week.

I went from having a nice cushion in my checking account and thinking 'I'm going to close the largest deal in the history of my business any week now' to owing high five figures at credit card interest rates while staring down an empty sales and product development pipeline.
Is optimizing the schedule of dentists' offices your passion? No? Then why are you committing to working on that for the next several years?
After 5 years of saying 'Thanks for the email Miriam. I'm sorry, Bingo Card Creator does not support using clip art. Have a nice day.' I was going absolutely spare.
My target for errors is zero, not 'so few that customers are unlikely to notice.'
key_insight: The most dangerous moment for a solo entrepreneur is when strategic transitions collide with financial obligations, because running multiple businesses on cash-basis accounting without a balance sheet means you can't see the cliff until you're already falling off it.
